nodes.title: Agent Forwarding
nodes.summary: Let a remote server request signatures from your local SSH agent—convenient, and dangerous in a very specific way.

nodes.title: authorized_keys
nodes.summary: The server-side trust anchor—the file of public keys a server will accept, and the discipline of auditing it.
entry_points: where does the server store my key
how does the server know my key

nodes.title: Blob
nodes.summary: The non-secret file on disk that a non-resident hardware key needs to reconstruct its private key. Not a secret—but lose it and the key is gone.
entry_points: what is the file on disk for a hardware key

nodes.title: Choosing an Approach
nodes.summary: The decision guide: disk-based key with passphrase, hardware-backed resident, or hardware-backed non-resident—chosen by situation, not by maximalism.
entry_points: which ssh key setup should I use
do I need a hardware key

nodes.title: Config Patterns and Wildcards
nodes.summary: Matching semantics—wildcards, per-network blocks, Host * global defaults, most-specific-match wins; plus the useful-options toolbox.

nodes.title: Deploying a Public Key
nodes.summary: Getting your public key onto the server—ssh-copy-id, the manual authorized_keys method, and the permissions that must accompany it.
entry_points: add my key to a server

nodes.title: FIDO2
nodes.summary: The cryptographic shift for the web—the server verifies a signature, not a secret; key-pair cryptography replacing shared secrets everywhere.

nodes.title: SSH File Permissions
nodes.summary: Why SSH is opinionated about permissions—a readable key is an untrustworthy key—the correct numbers on both sides, and the home-directory gotcha.
entry_points: why does ssh care about permissions

nodes.title: First-Time Key Setup
nodes.summary: The end-to-end quick start—generate, deploy with ssh-copy-id, test, add a config block; passwordless server access from zero.
entry_points: set up ssh keys for the first time
how do I stop typing my server password

nodes.title: Git over SSH
nodes.summary: The authentication-only model—no shell, no session; each git operation is a fresh cryptographic proof of identity. Same cryptography, different purpose.
entry_points: does github give me a shell

nodes.title: Hardware Key Capabilities
nodes.summary: The conceptual capability map—OTP, FIDO/U2F, WebAuthn, passkeys, SSH signing, PIV, OpenPGP—and how real models combine them.
entry_points: does the blue yubikey do ssh

nodes.title: Setting Up a Non-Resident Key
nodes.summary: Quick start for non-resident keys—generate ed25519-sk, register with GitHub/GitLab, config block, test—and the blob-backup obligation.

nodes.title: Setting Up a Resident Key
nodes.summary: Quick start for resident keys—generate with -O resident, PIN, deploy, test, and the critical step everyone skips: the duplicate on a second device.

nodes.title: Hardware Keys for SSH
nodes.summary: Hardware-backed SSH doesn't change the cryptography—it changes where authentication memory lives, splitting state between disk and device.
entry_points: why does hardware ssh feel weird

nodes.title: Hardware PINs
nodes.summary: A short code enforced by the device itself—attempt-limited, lockout-on-failure, impossible to brute-force offline.
entry_points: what happens if I forget my pin

nodes.title: Hardware Security Keys
nodes.summary: A physically enforced boundary where secrets can be used but never stolen—a cryptographic co-processor, not a USB password.
entry_points: what does a hardware security key do
how does a security key work

nodes.title: Host Aliases
nodes.summary: Host is a label you control, HostName is where the connection goes—SSH config as named connection instructions, not a hostname routing table.
entry_points: can host be a made-up name

nodes.title: Host Key Changed
nodes.summary: The scary warning decoded—legitimate causes (rebuilt server, reused IP) versus the attack it exists to catch, and the ssh-keygen -R fix.

nodes.title: IdentitiesOnly
nodes.summary: Force SSH to present only the keys you named—prevents SSH trying every key you own and the "too many authentication failures" it causes.

nodes.title: Configuring Jump Hosts
nodes.summary: The bastion pattern in ~/.ssh/config—ProxyJump blocks, wildcarded environment groups, transparent ssh prod-db1 through a bastion.
entry_points: configure a bastion in ssh config

nodes.title: Generating Keys
nodes.summary: ssh-keygen and its choices—algorithm, filename, comment, passphrase—plus changing the passphrase or comment on a key you already have.
entry_points: how to generate an ssh key

nodes.title: Naming Keys
nodes.summary: Names are for humans, not computers—clarity, intent, and scope in key filenames, plus why the comment field earns its keep.
entry_points: how should I name ssh keys
what is the ssh key comment

nodes.title: Key Pairs
nodes.summary: Two mathematically linked keys—private (secret, yours) and public (shared, safe)—and the storage rules that follow; the server never learns your secret.
entry_points: can I share my public key

nodes.title: Key Rotation
nodes.summary: Keys never expire on their own—rotation is a manual discipline: when to do it, the test-before-revoke order, and how hardware keys change the calculus.
entry_points: how often should I rotate ssh keys
left a job with keys on machines

nodes.title: Known Hosts
nodes.summary: The other direction of trust—you verify the server. Fingerprints, the first-connection prompt, and MITM protection.
entry_points: authenticity of host can't be established

nodes.title: Lost Hardware Key
nodes.summary: The recovery playbook when the device is gone—why non-resident loss is an inconvenience and resident-without-backup is total lockout.

nodes.title: macOS Keychain Integration
nodes.summary: Why SSH feels invisible on macOS—Keychain, Touch ID, and an auto-loaded agent. Feels like hardware security; is actually disk security plus OS trust.
entry_points: why doesn't my mac ask for my passphrase
is my mac key hardware backed

nodes.title: Managing Resident Credentials
nodes.summary: Housekeeping on the device—ykman list/delete, checking slot usage, freeing slots, and the slot-limit recovery path.

nodes.title: Multi-Factor Authentication
nodes.summary: Proof comes in categories—know, have, are—and MFA means requiring more than one. Includes the credential/factor vocabulary used everywhere else.

nodes.title: The MFA Strength Ladder
nodes.summary: The worst-to-best ranking—email link → SMS → TOTP → enterprise tokens → biometrics+WebAuthn → passkeys → hardware keys—and why each tier sits where it does.
entry_points: is sms worse than an authenticator app

nodes.title: The Modern Auth Landscape
nodes.summary: The map—passwords, OTP, FIDO2/WebAuthn, passkeys, and hardware keys all converge on one idea: never give the server a secret it can lose. SSH did it first.
entry_points: how do passkeys mfa and hardware keys fit together
how does ssh relate to webauthn
why do these all feel related

nodes.title: Multiple Accounts, One Service
nodes.summary: The two-accounts trap—same host, same username, different keys—and the alias-based way out, including clone-URL changes and updating existing remotes.

nodes.title: New Machine Setup
nodes.summary: Fresh laptop, restore access—three workflows side by side: regenerate stubs (resident), restore blobs (non-resident), restore keys + permissions (disk).

nodes.title: Non-Resident SSH Keys
nodes.summary: The key that is stored nowhere—derived on demand inside the hardware from master secret plus blob; unlimited keys, but you must preserve the blob.
entry_points: where is my sk key stored
the key isn't on the hardware?

nodes.title: One-Time Codes
nodes.summary: OTP/TOTP/SMS as layered defense on top of shared secrets—rotating codes help, but they remain phishable and replayable, and the secret still lives on a server.

nodes.title: Passkeys
nodes.summary: User-friendly FIDO2—discoverable credentials, synced by platforms, phishing-resistant; the tradeoff is that the platform controls creation, sync, and recovery.

nodes.title: Passphrases
nodes.summary: A secret that encrypts a private key at rest—and its honest limits: offline brute-force with unlimited attempts.

nodes.title: Passwords
nodes.summary: The shared-secret baseline—the server knows your secret, so the server can lose it. Everything else in modern authentication exists because of this flaw.

nodes.title: Permission Denied
nodes.summary: The most common SSH failure, and the diagnosis path: SSH silently refuses keys with wrong file permissions—no error, just "Permission denied."

nodes.title: Phishing Resistance
nodes.summary: Why cryptographic auth defeats phishing—nothing to type, nothing to replay; the authenticator verifies the service before signing. The thread tying SSH, FIDO2, and hardware keys together.
entry_points: why can't ssh keys be phished

nodes.title: PINs vs Passphrases
nodes.summary: A passphrase protects a file (offline brute-forceable, unlimited attempts); a PIN protects a physical device (rate-limited, hardware-enforced).
entry_points: is a short pin really safe
why is a 6 digit pin ok but a short passphrase not

nodes.title: Presence vs Identity
nodes.summary: Touch proves a human is present now; biometrics prove which human. For cryptographic auth, presence is sufficient and identity is optional.
entry_points: do I need the biometric yubikey
does my fingerprint go to the server

nodes.title: ProxyJump
nodes.summary: Route through an intermediate as a dumb TCP relay—it never sees your key, never talks to your agent, cannot act as you. The better answer to multi-hop.
entry_points: ssh through a jump host safely

nodes.title: Recovery Planning
nodes.summary: Always have a path back—the two-hardware-keys rule, blob backups, out-of-band access, and testing recovery before you need it.
entry_points: how do I not lock myself out

nodes.title: Resident SSH Keys
nodes.summary: The key the hardware remembers—generated and stored entirely inside the device, discoverable, survives disk loss; the cost is a scarce slot.
entry_points: ssh key that survives a wiped laptop

nodes.title: Resident vs Non-Resident
nodes.summary: The decision surface—permanence vs scalability. Resident trades slots for survivability; non-resident trades blob custody for unlimited keys. State survivability, not strength.
entry_points: which hardware key type should I use

nodes.title: Server Hardening
nodes.summary: Minimum viable server security—passwords off, root off, key-only access, sudo for escalation—and why keys structurally beat passwords.

nodes.title: Signatures
nodes.summary: The challenge–sign–verify flow—proof of possession that never reveals the secret. The missing piece that makes key pairs usable.
entry_points: how does the server verify me

nodes.title: Slots
nodes.summary: Small, protected storage locations inside the hardware key—intentionally scarce, because limiting stored secrets limits blast radius.
entry_points: what is a slot on a yubikey
how many keys fit on a hardware key

nodes.title: SSH Agents
nodes.summary: The background process that caches unlocked keys for a session so you stop retyping passphrases.
entry_points: why do I keep typing my passphrase

nodes.title: SSH Config Basics
nodes.summary: ~/.ssh/config as named connection instructions—Host, HostName, User, Port, IdentityFile—turning flag soup into `ssh myserver`.

nodes.title: Debugging SSH
nodes.summary: The diagnostic toolkit as a workflow—verbose output, config resolution, agent inspection, and server-side logs, each answering a different question.
entry_points: why is ssh using the wrong key

nodes.title: State
nodes.summary: Anything that must be remembered for authentication to keep working—lose it and auth fails, copy it and auth can be stolen.
entry_points: what must be remembered for ssh to work

nodes.title: Stolen Laptop
nodes.summary: Disk-key compromise response—assume the key is being brute-forced and remove the public key from every authorized_keys now.
entry_points: someone has my private key file

nodes.title: Stub
nodes.summary: The regenerable pointer—a small non-secret file that tells SSH which resident key to use. Delete it freely; regenerate anywhere with ssh-keygen -K.

nodes.title: The Touch Requirement
nodes.summary: A physical press required before any cryptographic operation—proves a human is present and the action is intentional; makes automation impossible.
entry_points: why do I have to touch my yubikey

nodes.title: Trust Boundaries
nodes.summary: Where the secret lives determines who you must trust—disk trusts your OS, hardware trusts a physical object, forwarding lends trust away.
entry_points: where does my ssh key live

nodes.title: The git@ Username
nodes.summary: Why the username is always git—the service identifies you by which key you present, not by the username you type.
entry_points: why is the username always git
how does github know who I am

nodes.title: WebAuthn
nodes.summary: The browser API that lets websites use FIDO2—the bridge between web pages and authenticators, phishing-resistant by design.
entry_points: how do browsers do passwordless login

nodes.title: What SSH Is
nodes.summary: SSH is cryptographic proof of identity over a hostile network—the protocol assumes someone is listening and someone is impersonating.
